docs.title: Ranking system
docs.text: Score Description 1 I have never heard of this skill or domain. 2 I have heard about this skill, but I don’t know how to use it or put it into practice. 3 I understand the basic concepts of this skill. 4 I can apply the basic concepts of this skill. 5 I can use this skill independently for simple and routine tasks. 6 I can apply this skill effectively for complex tasks and I am able to solve common problems without help. 7 I use this skill effectively in various complex situations and I can optimize processes or working methods. 8 I master this skill and I am able to train and mentor others in this field. 9 I am recognized as an internal expert, capable of developing new methods or improving existing practices thanks to this skill. 10 I am a recognized leader in this field, able to innovate and propose unique, cutting-edge solutions that set new standards.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Linux is widely distributed in the enterprise world. Linux administration involves a wide variety of tasks, from running web servers to configuring systems.

Fields like networking, game development, AI, and big data are rooted in Linux.

docs.title: The Kernel
docs.text:
Linux refers to the kernel, the brain of a system.

Some people refer to "Linux" as a collection of free software GNU/Linux (like Ubuntu), but behind it all, it's still the Linux kernel running at boot time that manages the system.

docs.title: UNIX History
docs.text:
Linux started with UNIX, an operating system developed by AT&T Bell Labs in the 1970s, written in C language (a low-level language).

Right now, Unix is a trademark and a specification owned by The Open Group.

docs.title: Birth of Linux
docs.text:
Over time, Unix was forked. In 1991, Linus Torvalds created a UNIX-like OS for educational use.

Despite adopting all the requirements of the UNIX specification, it has not been certified, so Linux isn't UNIX, just UNIX-like.

docs.title: The GNU Project
docs.text: Alongside, Richard Stallman in 1983 created the GNU Project. While trying to build their own OS, they realized they were better at building tools that match UNIX-like OS.

docs.title: 💡 Consider This
docs.text:
Linus originally named the project Freax, however, an administrator of the server where the development files were uploaded renamed it Linux, a portmanteau of Linus' name and UNIX. The name stuck.

GNU is a recursive acronym for "GNU's Not Unix," and it's pronounced just like the African horned antelope that is its namesake.

docs.title: Open Source
docs.text:
Most software has been issued with a closed source license: you can use the product but you don't have access to the source code, and sometimes you're required not to try to reverse engineer the source code.

The development of Linux follows the same path as Open Source: you have the right to obtain the software source code and modify it.

It was not the first software doing so, but since it was built from scratch, early adopters could influence the project greatly, accelerating the pace of development and preventing mistakes from other OS.

docs.title: 💡 Consider This
docs.text: The source code may be written in any of hundreds of different languages. Linux happens to be written in C, a versatile and relatively easy language to learn, which shares history with the original UNIX. This decision, made long before its utility was proven, turned out to be crucial in its nearly universal adoption as the primary operating system for internet servers.

docs.title: Linux Distributions
docs.text:
A distribution is a set of tools and suite of applications that come bundled together.

Like Linux and the GNU tools that add web browser and email client, some individuals and companies create their own distribution that takes care of installing the kernel, for example.

But they also include a package manager to help you manage software on your machine.

docs.title: Types of Distributions
docs.text: Some distributions focus on: - Running servers - Desktops - Specialized usage

docs.title: Major Players
docs.text:
The major players are: - Red Hat (RHEL, Fedora, CentOS Stream) - Debian (Ubuntu, Linux Mint) - SUSE (openSUSE) - Slackware

The visible differences can range from file locations to political philosophies.

docs.title: Command Line Interface (CLI)
docs.text: There are two ways of interacting with the computer: 1. GUI (Graphical User Interface) 2. CLI (Command Line Interface) - a text-based interface

docs.title: CLI Advantages
docs.text: Traditionally, OS offer both GUI and CLI interfaces. The CLI provides: - ✅ More precise control - ✅ Greater speed - ✅ Ability to automate tasks through scripting
